docs.title: cisshgo
docs.text: Simple, small, fast, concurrent SSH server to emulate network equipment (i.e. Cisco IOS) for testing purposes.

docs.title: What is cisshgo?
docs.text: cisshgo is a lightweight SSH server that emulates network devices like Cisco routers and switches. It's designed for testing network automation tools, scripts, and applications without requiring physical hardware or virtual machines.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Fast & Lightweight: Written in Go, minimal resource usage
  • Concurrent: Spawn multiple device instances simultaneously
  • Flexible: Support for multiple vendors and platforms (Cisco IOS/IOS-XR/NX-OS/ASA, Arista EOS, Juniper JunOS)
  • Customizable: Easy to add new commands and platforms via YAML configuration
  • Stateful: Scenario support for commands that change device state
  • Multi-device: Inventory system for complex network topologies
  • Template Support: Go templates in command outputs for dynamic responses

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Testing network automation scripts (Ansible, Netmiko, NAPALM)
  • CI/CD pipelines for network automation
  • Development and debugging without physical equipment
  • Training and demonstrations
  • Integration testing for network management tools

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Start 50 SSH listeners on ports 10000-10049
./cisshgo

# Connect to any device
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost

test_device#show version
Cisco IOS XE Software, Version 16.04.01
...

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT License - see LICENSE file for details.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: Cisco IOS is the property/trademark of Cisco Systems, Inc.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all cisshgo command-line flags and environment variables.

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --version -v - Show version information --listeners -l 50 Number of SSH listeners to spawn --starting-port -p 10000 Starting port number --transcript-map -t transcripts/transcript_map.yaml Path to transcript map file --platform -P csr1000v Platform to emulate --inventory -i - Path to inventory file (optional)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: cisshgo [flags]

docs.title: -v, --version
docs.text:
Show version information including commit hash and build date.

# Show version
./cisshgo --version
./cisshgo -v

Output: 

1.0.0 (commit: abc123def, built: 2026-03-01T12:00:00Z)

docs.title: -l, --listeners
docs.text:
Type: int Default: 50 Environment: CISSHGO_LISTENERS

Number of SSH listeners to spawn.

# Spawn 10 listeners
./cisshgo --listeners 10
./cisshgo -l 10

# Using environment variable
export CISSHGO_LISTENERS=10
./cisshgo

Ignored when using --inventory.

docs.title: -p, --starting-port
docs.text:
Type: int Default: 10000 Environment: CISSHGO_STARTING_PORT

Starting port number for SSH listeners. Listeners are spawned on sequential ports.

# Start at port 20000
./cisshgo --starting-port 20000
./cisshgo -p 20000

# Using environment variable
export CISSHGO_STARTING_PORT=20000
./cisshgo

With default settings (50 listeners, starting port 10000), listeners run on ports 10000-10049.

docs.title: -t, --transcript-map
docs.text:
Type: path Default: transcripts/transcript_map.yaml Environment: CISSHGO_TRANSCRIPT_MAP

Path to the transcript map file.

# Use custom transcript map
./cisshgo --transcript-map /path/to/custom_map.yaml
./cisshgo -t /path/to/custom_map.yaml

# Using environment variable
export CISSHGO_TRANSCRIPT_MAP=/path/to/custom_map.yaml
./cisshgo

See Configuration for transcript map format.

docs.title: -P, --platform
docs.text:
Type: string Default: csr1000v Environment: CISSHGO_PLATFORM

Platform to use when no inventory is provided. Must match a platform key in the transcript map.

# Use IOS platform
./cisshgo --platform ios
./cisshgo -P ios

# Using environment variable
export CISSHGO_PLATFORM=ios
./cisshgo

Ignored when using --inventory.

docs.title: -i, --inventory
docs.text:
Type: path Optional: Yes Environment: CISSHGO_INVENTORY

Path to inventory YAML file for multi-device topologies.

# Use inventory file
./cisshgo --inventory transcripts/inventory_example.yaml
./cisshgo -i transcripts/inventory_example.yaml

# Using environment variable
export CISSHGO_INVENTORY=transcripts/inventory_example.yaml
./cisshgo

When specified: - --listeners and --platform flags are ignored - Devices are spawned according to inventory configuration - Each device gets a sequential port starting from --starting-port

See Configuration for inventory format.

docs.title: Single Device
docs.text:
# One CSR1000v on port 10000
./cisshgo --listeners 1 --starting-port 10000 --platform csr1000v

docs.title: Multiple Platforms
docs.text:
# Use inventory for mixed topology
./cisshgo --inventory my_topology.yaml --starting-port 20000

Example my_topology.yaml:

devices:
  - platform: csr1000v
    count: 2
  - platform: ios
    count: 3
  - scenario: csr1000v-add-interface
    count: 1

This spawns 6 devices on ports 20000-20005.

docs.title: Custom Transcripts
docs.text:
# Use custom transcript map
./cisshgo --transcript-map /opt/transcripts/custom.yaml --platform ios

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
# Configure entirely via environment
export CISSHGO_LISTENERS=5
export CISSHGO_STARTING_PORT=30000
export CISSHGO_PLATFORM=nxos
export CISSHGO_TRANSCRIPT_MAP=/opt/transcripts/map.yaml
./cisshgo

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
# Single listener
docker run -d -p 10000:10000 \
  ghcr.io/tbotnz/cisshgo:latest \
  --listeners 1 --starting-port 10000

# Custom platform
docker run -d -p 10000-10009:10000-10009 \
  ghcr.io/tbotnz/cisshgo:latest \
  --listeners 10 --platform ios

# With custom transcripts (mount volume)
docker run -d -p 10000-10049:10000-10049 \
  -v /path/to/transcripts:/transcripts \
  ghcr.io/tbotnz/cisshgo:latest \
  --transcript-map /transcripts/custom.yaml

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
• 0 - Success
  • 1 - Configuration error (invalid flags, missing files, validation failure)
  • 2 - Runtime error (port binding failure, etc.)

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
cisshgo uses structured logging (slog) and logs to stderr:

2026/02/28 19:00:00 INFO Starting cisshgo listeners=50 startingPort=10000 platform=csr1000v
2026/02/28 19:00:00 INFO Listener started port=10000 platform=csr1000v
2026/02/28 19:00:00 INFO Listener started port=10001 platform=csr1000v
...

docs.title: Help
docs.text:
# Show help
./cisshgo -h
./cisshgo --help

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
cisshgo uses YAML files for configuration. There are two types of configuration files:

  1. Transcript map - Defines platforms, commands, and CLI contexts
  2. Inventory - Defines multi-device topologies

docs.title: Transcript Map
docs.text: The transcript map file (transcripts/transcript_map.yaml) defines available platforms and their command responses.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
platforms:
  csr1000v:
    vendor: "cisco"
    hostname: "cisshgo1000v"
    password: "admin"
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_version.txt"
      "show ip interface brief": "transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_ip_interface_brief.txt"
    context_hierarchy:
      "(config)#": "#"
      "#": ">"
      ">": "exit"
    context_search:
      "configure terminal": "(config)#"
      "enable": "#"
      "base": ">"

docs.title: Platform Key
docs.text: The top-level key (e.g., csr1000v) is the platform identifier used with --platform flag or in inventory files.

docs.title: vendor
docs.text: The device vendor (e.g., cisco, arista, juniper).

docs.title: hostname
docs.text: The hostname displayed in the CLI prompt. Supports Go template variables (see Transcripts).

docs.title: username
docs.text:
Optional. The SSH username required to authenticate. When set, cisshgo enforces both username and password — connections with a different username are rejected. When omitted, any username is accepted (only the password is checked).

junos:
  username: "admin"   # only "admin" can connect
  password: "admin"

Also available as {{.Username}} in transcript templates.

docs.title: password
docs.text: SSH password for authentication.

docs.title: prompt_format
docs.text:
Optional. A format string controlling how the CLI prompt is constructed. When omitted, the prompt defaults to {hostname}{context} (standard Cisco style).

Available variables:

Variable Value {hostname} Device hostname (changes with hostname command) {username} Configured username (from username field) {context} Current CLI context suffix (e.g., >, #, (config)#) 
# Cisco style (default — no prompt_format needed)
csr1000v:
  hostname: "router"
  # prompt renders as: router>

# Junos style
junos:
  hostname: "mx240"
  username: "admin"
  prompt_format: "{username}@{hostname}{context}"
  # prompt renders as: admin@mx240>

docs.title: context_prefix_lines
docs.text:
Optional. A map of context values to prefix lines displayed above the prompt. Used for platforms like Junos that show [edit] above the prompt in configuration mode.

junos:
  context_search:
    "base": "> "
    "configure": "# "
  context_prefix_lines:
    "# ": "[edit]"   # shown above prompt when in config mode

When in config mode, the terminal renders: 

[edit]
admin@mx240#

docs.title: command_transcripts
docs.text:
Maps CLI commands to transcript files. Commands are matched using fuzzy matching (see Transcripts).

command_transcripts:
  "show version": "transcripts/cisco/ios/show_version.txt"
  "show running-config": "transcripts/cisco/ios/show_running-config.txt"
  "terminal length 0": "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"

Paths are relative to the transcript map file location.

docs.title: context_hierarchy
docs.text:
Defines the CLI context hierarchy and how to navigate between contexts.

context_hierarchy:
  "(config)#": "#"      # From config mode, go to enable mode
  "#": ">"              # From enable mode, go to user mode
  ">": "exit"           # From user mode, exit

docs.title: context_search
docs.text:
Maps commands to the context they enter.

context_search:
  "configure terminal": "(config)#"
  "enable": "#"
  "base": ">"

The base key defines the initial context when a user connects.

docs.title: Scenarios
docs.text: Scenarios enable stateful command responses that simulate device configuration changes. Unlike static platforms, scenarios define a sequence of commands that, when executed in order, change the device's responses to subsequent commands.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Scenarios are defined in a separate scenarios: section at the same level as platforms::

scenarios:
  csr1000v-add-interface:
    platform: csr1000v
    sequence:
      - command: "show running-config"
        transcript: "transcripts/scenarios/csr1000v-add-interface/running_config_before.txt"
      - command: "interface GigabitEthernet0/0/2"
        transcript: "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"
      - command: "ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.255.0"
        transcript: "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"
      - command: "end"
        transcript: "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"
      - command: "show running-config"
        transcript: "transcripts/scenarios/csr1000v-add-interface/running_config_after.txt"

docs.title: Fields
docs.text:
platform

The base platform identifier from the platforms: section. The scenario inherits all settings (vendor, hostname, password, contexts) from this platform.

sequence

An ordered list of command/transcript pairs. Each step defines: - command: The exact command to match - transcript: The transcript file to return for that command

docs.title: How Scenarios Work
docs.text:
1. Initial State: When a scenario starts, the first command in the sequence is active
  2. Command Execution: When a user executes a command that matches the current sequence step, that transcript is returned
  3. State Progression: After a sequence command is executed, the scenario advances to the next step
  4. State Changes: Subsequent commands (like the second show running-config) return different transcripts, simulating configuration changes
  5. Non-sequence Commands: Commands not in the sequence fall back to the base platform's command_transcripts

docs.title: Example Flow
docs.text:
Using the csr1000v-add-interface scenario above:

1. User: show running-config
   → Returns: running_config_before.txt (no GigabitEthernet0/0/2)

2. User: interface GigabitEthernet0/0/2
   → Returns: empty (enters config mode)

3. User: ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.255.0
   → Returns: empty (configures IP)

4. User: end
   → Returns: empty (exits config mode)

5. User: show running-config
   → Returns: running_config_after.txt (now includes GigabitEthernet0/0/2 with IP)

The device appears to have been configured, even though it's just playing back different transcripts.

docs.title: Scenario Behavior
docs.text:
Out-of-sequence commands: Commands must be executed in the exact order defined in the sequence. Executing commands out of order or skipping steps will not advance the sequence state.

Non-sequence commands: Commands not in the sequence (like show version) work normally using the base platform's command transcripts.

Sequence completion: Once all sequence steps are executed, the scenario remains in its final state. The sequence does not reset or loop.

docs.title: Running Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenarios are used via inventory files. Create an inventory file:

devices:
  - scenario: csr1000v-add-interface
    count: 1

Then run cisshgo:

./cisshgo --inventory my_inventory.yaml --starting-port 10000

Connect and execute the sequence:

ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost
# Execute commands in order to progress through the scenario

docs.title: Inventory
docs.text: The inventory file defines a multi-device topology with different platforms and counts.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
devices:
  - platform: csr1000v
    count: 2
  - platform: ios
    count: 3
  - scenario: csr1000v-add-interface
    count: 1

docs.title: platform
docs.text: The platform identifier from the transcript map.

docs.title: scenario
docs.text:
A scenario identifier (also defined in the transcript map under scenarios:). Mutually exclusive with platform.

Scenarios are stateful command sequences - see Scenarios for details.

docs.title: count
docs.text: Number of listeners to spawn for this platform/scenario.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
./cisshgo --inventory transcripts/inventory_example.yaml

When using an inventory file: - The --listeners flag is ignored - Listeners are spawned sequentially starting from --starting-port - Each device gets a unique port

Example with 6 total devices starting at port 10000: - Ports 10000-10001: csr1000v (2 devices) - Ports 10002-10004: ios (3 devices) - Port 10005: csr1000v-add-interface scenario (1 device)

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
Create my_lab.yaml:

devices:
  - platform: csr1000v
    count: 2
  - platform: ios
    count: 1
  - scenario: csr1000v-add-interface
    count: 1

Run cisshgo:

./cisshgo --inventory my_lab.yaml --starting-port 10000

Connect to devices:

# CSR1000v devices
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost
ssh -p 10001 admin@localhost

# IOS device
ssh -p 10002 admin@localhost

# Scenario device (stateful)
ssh -p 10003 admin@localhost

docs.title: Path Resolution
docs.text: All paths in the transcript map are resolved relative to the transcript map file location, not the current working directory. This allows the transcript map to be placed anywhere without breaking relative paths.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
cisshgo validates the transcript map at startup:

  • All referenced transcript files must exist
  • Platform/scenario references in inventory must exist in transcript map
  • Inventory entries must specify exactly one of platform or scenario

If validation fails, cisshgo exits with an error before spawning any listeners.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thanks for your interest in contributing to cisshgo! This guide covers development setup, workflow, and how to add new features.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
Prerequisites: Go 1.26+

git clone https://github.com/tbotnz/cisshgo
cd cisshgo
go build ./...
go test ./...

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
cisshgo uses GitHub Flow:

  1. Fork the repo and create a branch from master
  2. Make your changes
  3. Ensure tests pass and coverage stays above 90%
  4. Ensure code is formatted with gofmt
  5. Open a pull request against master

docs.title: Branch Naming
docs.text:
• feat/short-description — new features
  • fix/short-description — bug fixes
  • docs/short-description — documentation
  • refactor/short-description — refactoring
  • chore/short-description — maintenance

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Follow Conventional Commits:

feat: add show interfaces command for csr1000v
fix: handle empty input in exec mode
docs: update CLI reference
chore: update dependencies

Conventional commits are required — they feed into automated CHANGELOG.md generation via git-cliff. Non-conventional commits are excluded from the changelog.

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# All tests with race detection
go test -race ./...

# With coverage report
go test -coverprofile=coverage.out -covermode=atomic ./...
go tool cover -func=coverage.out

# View coverage in browser
go tool cover -html=coverage.out

Coverage requirement: 90% minimum. The CI workflow enforces this on every PR.

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
# Format code
gofmt -w ./...

# Run linter (if golangci-lint is installed)
golangci-lint run

docs.title: Adding Command Transcripts
docs.text: The simplest contribution is adding output for a new command on an existing platform.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Create a plain text file in transcripts/vendor/platform/:
# Example: add "show interfaces" for IOS
vim transcripts/cisco/ios/show_interfaces.txt
  1. Add the command to the transcript map file transcripts/transcript_map.yaml:
platforms:
  ios:
    command_transcripts:
      "show interfaces": "transcripts/cisco/ios/show_interfaces.txt"
  1. Test it:
./cisshgo --platform ios --listeners 1
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost
# Try: show interfaces

docs.title: Using Templates
docs.text:
Transcripts support Go templates for dynamic content:

{{.Hostname}} uptime is 4 hours, 55 minutes
Processor board ID {{.Platform}}-12345

Available variables: Hostname, Vendor, Platform, Password

See Transcripts for details.

docs.title: For Cisco-style Devices
docs.text:
1. Create transcript directory:
mkdir -p transcripts/vendor/platform
  1. Add transcript files for common commands:
  2. show_version.txt
  3. show_running-config.txt

  4. show_ip_interface_brief.txt (or equivalent)

  5. Add platform entry to the transcript map file (transcript_map.yaml):

platforms:
  new_platform:
    vendor: "vendor_name"
    hostname: "device-hostname"
    password: "admin"
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/vendor/platform/show_version.txt"
      "terminal length 0": "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"
    context_hierarchy:
      "(config)#": "#"
      "#": ">"
      ">": "exit"
    context_search:
      "configure terminal": "(config)#"
      "enable": "#"
      "base": ">"
  1. Add test in fakedevices/genericFakeDevice_test.go:
func TestNewPlatformInitialization(t *testing.T) {
    // Test platform loads correctly
}
  1. Test the platform:
./cisshgo --platform new_platform --listeners 1
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost

docs.title: For Non-Cisco Devices
docs.text: Devices with different CLI patterns (Juniper, F5, etc.) require a custom handler in ssh_server/handlers/. This is an advanced topic — open an issue to discuss the approach before implementing.

docs.title: Adding Tests
docs.text:
When adding new features, include tests:

func TestNewFeature(t *testing.T) {
    // Arrange
    input := "test input"

    // Act
    result := NewFeature(input)

    // Assert
    if result != expected {
        t.Errorf("got %v, want %v", result, expected)
    }
}

Run tests frequently during development:

go test -v ./...

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Update documentation when adding features:

  • README.md - Quick start and overview
  • docs/ - Detailed documentation (this site)
  • Code comments - Exported functions and types
  • CHANGELOG.md - Automatically generated from commits

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
Open a GitHub issue. For bugs, include:

  • cisshgo version (e.g., v0.2.0)
  • SSH client and command used
  • Expected vs actual behavior
  • Relevant logs

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Issues: github.com/tbotnz/cisshgo/issues
  • Discussions: github.com/tbotnz/cisshgo/discussions

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: Be respectful and constructive. We're all here to build something useful.

docs.title: Pre-built Binaries
docs.text:
Download the latest release from GitHub Releases:

# Download for your platform (linux/darwin/windows, amd64/arm64)
wget https://github.com/tbotnz/cisshgo/releases/latest/download/cisshgo_linux_amd64.tar.gz
tar -xzf cisshgo_linux_amd64.tar.gz
./cisshgo

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
Pull and run the latest release:

docker run -d -p 10000-10049:10000-10049 ghcr.io/tbotnz/cisshgo:latest

Run with custom options:

docker run -d -p 10000:10000 ghcr.io/tbotnz/cisshgo:latest --listeners 1 --starting-port 10000

docs.title: Building from Source
docs.text:
Requirements: Go 1.26+

git clone https://github.com/tbotnz/cisshgo
cd cisshgo
go build -o cisshgo cissh.go
./cisshgo

Or run directly:

go run cissh.go

docs.title: Start the Server
docs.text:
Default configuration (50 listeners on ports 10000-10049):

./cisshgo

Single listener on port 10000:

./cisshgo --listeners 1 --starting-port 10000

docs.title: Connect to a Device
docs.text:
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost

Default password: admin

Note: The hostname shown in the prompt (e.g., cisshgo1000v#) is determined by the platform's configuration in the transcript map file. The default platform is csr1000v with hostname cisshgo1000v.

docs.title: Example Session
docs.text:
$ ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost
admin@localhost's password: 
test_device>enable
test_device#show version
Cisco IOS XE Software, Version 16.04.01
Cisco IOS Software [Everest], CSR1000V Software (X86_64_LINUX_IOSD-UNIVERSALK9-M), Version 16.4.1, RELEASE SOFTWARE (fc2)
Technical Support: http://www.cisco.com/techsupport
Copyright (c) 1986-2016 by Cisco Systems, Inc.
Compiled Sun 27-Nov-16 13:02 by mcpre

test_device#show ip interface brief
Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status                Protocol
GigabitEthernet1       10.0.0.1        YES NVRAM  up                    up      
GigabitEthernet2       unassigned      YES NVRAM  administratively down down    

test_device#configure terminal
test_device(config)#exit
test_device#

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
All CLI flags can be set via environment variables:

export CISSHGO_LISTENERS=10
export CISSHGO_STARTING_PORT=20000
export CISSHGO_PLATFORM=ios
./cisshgo

See CLI Reference for all available options.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration - Learn about transcript map file and inventory files
  • Transcripts - Customize command outputs and add new commands
  • CLI Reference - Complete CLI flag documentation

docs.title: Migration Guide: v0.2.0 to v1.0.0
docs.text: This guide covers all breaking changes and new features introduced in v1.0.0.

docs.title: 1. CLI Flag Names
docs.text:
All flags now use kebab-case (double-dash) instead of camelCase (single-dash).

v0.2.0 v1.0.0 Short flag -startingPort --starting-port -p -transcriptMap --transcript-map -t -listeners --listeners -l

Before: 

./cisshgo -startingPort 10000 -transcriptMap custom.yaml -listeners 10

After: 

./cisshgo --starting-port 10000 --transcript-map custom.yaml --listeners 10

All flags can also be set via environment variables:

CISSHGO_STARTING_PORT=10000 CISSHGO_TRANSCRIPT_MAP=custom.yaml ./cisshgo

docs.title: 2. Transcript Map YAML Schema
docs.text:
The platforms section changed from a list of single-key maps to a plain map.

Before (v0.2.0): 

platforms:
  - csr1000v:
      vendor: "cisco"
      hostname: "router"
      password: "admin"
      command_transcripts:
        "show version": "transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_version.txt"
      context_search:
        "enable": "#"
        "base": ">"
      context_hierarchy:
        "#": ">"
        ">": "exit"

After (v1.0.0): 

platforms:
  csr1000v:
    vendor: "cisco"
    hostname: "router"
    password: "admin"
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_version.txt"
    context_search:
      "enable": "#"
      "base": ">"
    context_hierarchy:
      "#": ">"
      ">": "exit"

See the migration script to automate this conversion.

docs.title: 3. Transcript Paths Resolved Relative to Transcript Map File
docs.text:
In v0.2.0, transcript paths were resolved relative to the working directory of the process. In v1.0.0, they are resolved relative to the directory containing the transcript map file.

If you run cisshgo from outside the repo root, update your transcript paths accordingly, or use absolute paths.

docs.title: Inventory System (--inventory)
docs.text:
Define multi-device topologies in a YAML file instead of spawning identical listeners:

# inventory.yaml
devices:
  - platform: csr1000v
    count: 10
  - platform: nxos
    count: 5

./cisshgo --inventory inventory.yaml --transcript-map transcript_map.yaml

docs.title: --platform Flag
docs.text:
When not using an inventory file, specify which platform to use (default: csr1000v):

./cisshgo --platform nxos --listeners 5

docs.title: Scenario-Based Stateful Responses
docs.text:
Define ordered command/response sequences for testing stateful workflows:

scenarios:
  add-interface:
    platform: csr1000v
    sequence:
      - command: "show running-config"
        transcript: "transcripts/before.txt"
      - command: "interface GigabitEthernet0/0/2"
        transcript: "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"
      - command: "show running-config"
        transcript: "transcripts/after.txt"

Reference scenarios in your inventory:

devices:
  - scenario: add-interface
    count: 3

docs.title: Username Enforcement
docs.text:
Optionally enforce a specific SSH username per platform:

platforms:
  junos:
    username: "admin"   # only "admin" can connect
    password: "admin"

docs.title: Flexible Prompt Formatting
docs.text:
Use prompt_format for non-Cisco prompt styles:

platforms:
  junos:
    username: "admin"
    prompt_format: "{username}@{hostname}{context}"
    # renders as: admin@hostname>

docs.title: Multi-line Prompts (context_prefix_lines)
docs.text:
For platforms like Junos that show a line above the prompt in config mode:

platforms:
  junos:
    context_prefix_lines:
      "#": "[edit]"
    # config mode renders as:
    # [edit]
    # admin@hostname#

docs.title: Additional Platform Transcripts
docs.text:
v1.0.0 ships with transcripts for 7 platforms out of the box:

Platform Vendor csr1000v Cisco IOS XE ios Cisco IOS iosxr Cisco IOS XR asa Cisco ASA nxos Cisco NX-OS eos Arista EOS junos Juniper Junos

docs.title: Graceful Shutdown
docs.text: cisshgo now handles SIGINT/SIGTERM and shuts down all listeners cleanly.

docs.title: --version Flag
docs.text: ./cisshgo --version

docs.title: Migration Script
docs.text:
A Python script is provided to automate the transcript map schema migration:

# Requires Python 3 and PyYAML (pip install pyyaml)

# Preview output:
python3 scripts/migrate_transcript_map.py transcript_map.yaml

# Migrate in-place:
python3 scripts/migrate_transcript_map.py transcript_map.yaml > /tmp/map.yaml && mv /tmp/map.yaml transcript_map.yaml

The script handles: - v0.2.0 list-of-maps → v1.0.0 map (migrates) - Already v1.0.0 format (no-op, reports "no changes needed") - Empty platforms list → empty map - Missing platforms key → no change

docs.title: Step-by-Step Upgrade Checklist
docs.text:
1. Update CLI flags in any scripts or tooling (see CLI flag table)
  2. Migrate transcript map from list-of-maps to map format (see migration script or schema change)
  3. Verify transcript paths resolve correctly from the transcript map file's directory
  4. Optionally adopt inventory files, scenarios, or new platform transcripts

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
unknown flag: -startingPort → Rename to --starting-port (see CLI flag names)

parsing transcript map: yaml: unmarshal errors → Your transcript map uses the old list-of-maps format. Run the migration script or update manually.

transcript map validation failed: platform "X" command "Y": <path> → Transcript paths are now resolved relative to the transcript map file. Check that paths are correct relative to where your transcript_map.yaml lives.

platform "csr1000v" not found in transcript map → The --platform flag defaults to csr1000v. If your transcript map uses a different platform name, pass --platform <name>.

docs.title: Transcripts
docs.text: Transcripts are plain text files containing command output. They support Go templates for dynamic content.

docs.title: Basic Transcript
docs.text:
Create a plain text file with the command output:

Cisco IOS XE Software, Version 16.04.01
Cisco IOS Software [Everest], CSR1000V Software (X86_64_LINUX_IOSD-UNIVERSALK9-M), Version 16.4.1, RELEASE SOFTWARE (fc2)
Technical Support: http://www.cisco.com/techsupport
Copyright (c) 1986-2016 by Cisco Systems, Inc.
Compiled Sun 27-Nov-16 13:02 by mcpre

Save it in the appropriate vendor/platform directory:

transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_version.txt

docs.title: Adding to Transcript Map
docs.text:
Add an entry in transcripts/transcript_map.yaml:

platforms:
  csr1000v:
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/cisco/csr1000v/show_version.txt"

docs.title: Go Templates
docs.text: Transcripts support Go template syntax for dynamic content.

docs.title: Available Variables
docs.text:
The FakeDevice struct provides these template variables:

type FakeDevice struct {
    Vendor            string            // Device vendor (e.g., "cisco")
    Platform          string            // Platform identifier (e.g., "csr1000v")
    Hostname          string            // Device hostname
    Username          string            // SSH username (from platform config)
    Password          string            // SSH password
    SupportedCommands SupportedCommands // Available commands
    ContextSearch     map[string]string // CLI contexts
    ContextHierarchy  map[string]string // Context navigation
}

docs.title: Template Example
docs.text:
ROM: IOS-XE ROMMON
{{.Hostname}} uptime is 4 hours, 55 minutes
Uptime for this control processor is 4 hours, 56 minutes
System returned to ROM by reload
System image file is "bootflash:packages.conf"
Last reload reason: reload

When rendered with hostname cisshgo1000v:

ROM: IOS-XE ROMMON
cisshgo1000v uptime is 4 hours, 55 minutes
Uptime for this control processor is 4 hours, 56 minutes
System returned to ROM by reload
System image file is "bootflash:packages.conf"
Last reload reason: reload

docs.title: Using Templates in Practice
docs.text:
1. Create a transcript file transcripts/cisco/ios/show_version.txt:
Cisco IOS Software, {{.Platform}} Software
ROM: Bootstrap program is IOS
{{.Hostname}} uptime is 1 day, 2 hours, 30 minutes
  1. Reference it in the transcript map:
platforms:
  ios:
    hostname: "my-router"
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/cisco/ios/show_version.txt"
  1. Run and test:
./cisshgo --platform ios --listeners 1
ssh -p 10000 admin@localhost

Output will show: 

Cisco IOS Software, ios Software
ROM: Bootstrap program is IOS
my-router uptime is 1 day, 2 hours, 30 minutes

docs.title: Template Functions
docs.text:
Standard Go template functions are available (see text/template documentation for the full reference):

  • String manipulation: printf, print, println
  • Conditionals: if, else, end
  • Loops: range, end
  • Comparisons: eq, ne, lt, le, gt, ge

Example with conditionals:

{{if eq .Platform "csr1000v"}}
Cisco CSR1000V (VXE) processor with 2070375K/3075K bytes of memory.
{{else}}
Cisco IOS processor with 1048576K bytes of memory.
{{end}}

docs.title: Command Matching
docs.text:
Commands are matched using fuzzy matching, which allows for:

  • Partial commands: sh ver matches show version
  • Abbreviations: conf t matches configure terminal
  • Extra whitespace: show version matches show version

docs.title: Matching Algorithm
docs.text:
The fuzzy matcher:

  1. Normalizes whitespace
  2. Splits command into tokens
  3. Matches each token as a prefix
  4. Returns the best match if unambiguous

docs.title: Ambiguous Commands
docs.text:
If multiple commands match, cisshgo returns an error:

test_device#sh
% Ambiguous command: "sh"

To avoid ambiguity, ensure command prefixes are unique within each context.

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text: cisshgo includes transcripts for these platforms:

docs.title: Cisco
docs.text:
• IOS (ios) - Classic Cisco IOS
  • IOS-XE (csr1000v) - Cisco CSR1000v
  • IOS-XR (iosxr) - Cisco IOS-XR
  • NX-OS (nxos) - Cisco Nexus
  • ASA (asa) - Cisco ASA Firewall

docs.title: Arista
docs.text: • EOS (eos) - Arista EOS

docs.title: Juniper
docs.text: • JunOS (junos) - Juniper JunOS

docs.title: Cisco-style Platforms
docs.text:
For devices with Cisco-like CLI (enable mode, config mode, etc.):

  1. Create transcript directory: transcripts/vendor/platform/
  2. Add transcript files for common commands
  3. Add platform entry to transcript_map.yaml

Example:

platforms:
  new_platform:
    vendor: "cisco"
    hostname: "new-device"
    password: "admin"
    command_transcripts:
      "show version": "transcripts/cisco/new_platform/show_version.txt"
    context_hierarchy:
      "(config)#": "#"
      "#": ">"
      ">": "exit"
    context_search:
      "configure terminal": "(config)#"
      "enable": "#"
      "base": ">"

docs.title: Non-Cisco Platforms
docs.text: For devices with different CLI patterns (e.g., Juniper, F5), you'll need to implement a custom handler in the ssh_server/handlers package. This is an advanced topic - see the Contributing guide for details.

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Most platforms should support these basic commands:

  • show version - Device version information
  • show running-config - Current configuration
  • show ip interface brief - Interface status (Cisco)
  • show interfaces - Interface details (Juniper)
  • terminal length 0 - Disable pagination
  • enable - Enter privileged mode
  • configure terminal - Enter configuration mode
  • exit - Exit current mode

docs.title: Empty Returns
docs.text:
For commands that produce no output (like terminal length 0), use the generic empty return:

command_transcripts:
  "terminal length 0": "transcripts/generic_empty_return.txt"

The file should be empty or contain only whitespace.

docs.title: Template Errors
docs.text:
If you see errors like template: ...: executing ... at <.InvalidField>: can't evaluate field:

  • Check that the field name matches exactly (case-sensitive)
  • Available fields: Vendor, Platform, Hostname, Username, Password
  • Use {{.Hostname}} not {{.hostname}}

docs.title: Command Not Found
docs.text:
If cisshgo returns "Command not found" or no output:

  • Verify the command is spelled correctly in transcript_map.yaml
  • Check that the command is defined for the current CLI context
  • Try the full command - fuzzy matching may not work for very short inputs (e.g., s alone)
  • Use show version instead of just sh if ambiguous

docs.title: Ambiguous Command Errors
docs.text:
If you see % Ambiguous command:

  • Multiple commands match your input
  • Use more characters to make the command unique
  • Example: sh matches both show and shutdown - use sho or show

docs.title: File Not Found Errors
docs.text:
If cisshgo fails to start with "file not found" or "no such file":

  • Check that all transcript paths in transcript_map.yaml are correct
  • Paths are relative to the transcript map file location, not your current directory
  • Verify files exist: ls -la transcripts/cisco/ios/show_version.txt
  • Check for typos in filenames

docs.title: Connection Refused
docs.text:
If SSH connection fails:

  • Verify cisshgo is running: check for "Listener started" log messages
  • Check the port number matches: ssh -p 10000 for default starting port
  • Ensure no firewall is blocking the port
  • Try netstat -tuln | grep 10000 to verify the port is listening
